# SECTION 15 — FUNCTIONAL SPECIALTY PANELS
## Translation Key: Laboratory Tests Mapped to Body Codx Operations

---

## NutrEval FMV (Functional Micronutrient-Vitamin Panel)

**Body Codx Operation:** Cross-Operation
**What It Measures:** 125+ biomarkers spanning vitamins, minerals, amino acids, fatty acids, antioxidants, organic acids, and oxidative stress markers — the single most comprehensive functional medicine panel available.
**Sample Type:** Blood (plasma/serum) + Urine (first morning void)
**Fasting Required:** Yes (10–12 hours)
**Lab Provider:** Genova Diagnostics
**Test Code:** GDX-NE
**Estimated Cost:** $800–$1,200
**Insurance Coverage:** Rarely covered; some functional medicine plans reimburse partially
**Turnaround Time:** 10–14 business days
**Availability:** Physician order required

### What It Measures

The NutrEval FMV is not a single test — it is an integrated metabolic surveillance platform that simultaneously reads the functional status of virtually every micronutrient pathway in the body. [FACT] The panel quantifies fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), water-soluble vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B5, B6, B12, folate, vitamin C), minerals (magnesium, zinc, copper, iron, manganese, selenium, chromium, molybdenum), 21 plasma amino acids, essential and non-essential fatty acids (omega-3 index, omega-6/omega-3 ratio, EPA, DHA, arachidonic acid, linoleic acid), antioxidants (CoQ10, alpha-lipoic acid, carnitine, glutathione), organic acids reflecting Krebs cycle efficiency and B-vitamin-dependent enzyme function, and oxidative stress markers including lipid peroxides and 8-hydroxy-2-deoxyguanosine (8-OHdG). [FACT]

What makes the NutrEval fundamentally different from conventional nutrient panels is its functional methodology. [INTERPRETATION] Standard lab testing measures the amount of a nutrient circulating in the blood — a snapshot of supply. The NutrEval adds functional metabolic markers that measure whether the nutrient is actually being used by the enzymes that require it. For example, serum B12 may appear "normal" at 350 pg/mL, but if methylmalonic acid (MMA) is elevated in the urine, the body is telling you that B12 is not reaching its enzymatic targets — it is circulating but not functioning. [FACT] Similarly, serum folate may be adequate, but elevated formiminoglutamic acid (FIGlu) reveals that folate-dependent histidine metabolism is impaired. [FACT] The NutrEval reads both the nutrient and its metabolic consequence, giving the clinician a map of nutritional throughput rather than merely nutritional supply.

Because the panel spans vitamins, minerals, amino acids, fatty acids, organic acids, and oxidative stress, it does not belong to any single Light Machine operation. It reads across all eight simultaneously: Reception (are nutrients arriving from the diet?), Transduction (are they being absorbed and converted?), Synthesis (are they being assembled into the molecules the body needs?), Conduction (are they reaching target tissues?), Regulation (are feedback loops adjusting nutrient utilization?), Defense (is oxidative stress overwhelming antioxidant capacity?), Restoration (are repair substrates available?), and Elimination (are metabolic waste products being cleared?). [INTERPRETATION] This cross-operational reach is what makes the NutrEval the single most diagnostically dense order a clinician can write.

### Ranges

Because the NutrEval reports 125+ individual biomarkers, each with its own reference and functional optimal range, a comprehensive ranges table is not practical here. Instead, the critical interpretive framework:

| Reporting Level | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **"Needs" (Red zone)** | Functional deficiency — enzymatic function measurably impaired; intervention priority [FACT] |
| **"Borderline" (Yellow zone)** | Suboptimal — enzyme function beginning to decline, compensatory mechanisms engaged [FACT] |
| **"Adequate" (Green zone)** | Functional sufficiency — metabolic pathways operating within normal efficiency [FACT] |

**Key functional markers and their optimal thresholds:**

| Functional Marker | What It Reads | Optimal Range |
|---|---|---|
| Methylmalonic acid (MMA) | Functional B12 status | <3.8 µg/mg creatinine [FACT] |
| Formiminoglutamic acid (FIGlu) | Functional folate status | <2.0 µg/mg creatinine [FACT] |
| Xanthurenate | Functional B6 status | <0.7 µg/mg creatinine [FACT] |
| 8-OHdG | DNA oxidative damage | <5.0 ng/mg creatinine [INTERPRETATION] |
| Lipid peroxides | Membrane oxidative damage | <3.0 nmol/mL [INTERPRETATION] |
| Omega-3 Index (EPA+DHA) | Cell membrane omega-3 incorporation | >8% [FACT — based on Harris & Von Schacky cardioprotection data] |
| Omega-6/Omega-3 ratio | Inflammatory vs. resolving substrate balance | <3:1 functional optimal; <5:1 acceptable [INTERPRETATION] |
| CoQ10 | Mitochondrial electron transport capacity | >0.9 µg/mL [INTERPRETATION] |
| Glutathione (reduced) | Master antioxidant reservoir | >200 µmol/L [INTERPRETATION] |

### What Deviation Signals

**Widespread "Needs" across multiple categories** indicates systemic nutritional failure that transcends any single dietary insufficiency. [INTERPRETATION] When fat-soluble vitamins, water-soluble vitamins, and minerals are simultaneously depleted, the clinician should suspect impaired absorption rather than inadequate intake — particularly in patients who report "eating well." The most common upstream causes of multi-nutrient depletion are gastric acid suppression (PPIs reduce absorption of B12, iron, magnesium, calcium, and zinc), intestinal inflammation (celiac disease, Crohn's, SIBO), pancreatic exocrine insufficiency, and gut dysbiosis interfering with nutrient biotransformation. [FACT] A NutrEval showing multi-category deficiency in someone with adequate dietary intake is a Transduction operation alarm: the machine is receiving raw material but cannot convert it into usable form.

**Amino acid imbalances** on the NutrEval reveal Synthesis operation dysfunction at the most fundamental level. [INTERPRETATION] Low essential amino acids (leucine, isoleucine, valine, tryptophan, phenylalanine, methionine, threonine, lysine) indicate protein maldigestion, inadequate protein intake, or impaired intestinal amino acid transport. Low branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs: leucine, isoleucine, valine) specifically signal poor anabolic capacity — the body's building operation is substrate-limited. [FACT] Abnormal tryptophan with elevated kynurenine metabolites on the organic acids section suggests immune-driven tryptophan diversion away from serotonin synthesis and toward the kynurenine pathway, producing quinolinic acid (neurotoxic) at the expense of serotonin and melatonin production. [FACT] This pattern — low tryptophan, high kynurenine metabolites — is the biochemical signature of inflammation hijacking neurotransmitter synthesis, and it bridges Defense (inflammation) and Synthesis (neurotransmitter production) in a single finding.

**Elevated oxidative stress markers with depleted antioxidants** is the NutrEval's most urgent alarm pattern. [INTERPRETATION] When 8-OHdG is elevated (indicating DNA damage from reactive oxygen species), lipid peroxides are elevated (indicating cell membrane damage), and simultaneously glutathione, CoQ10, and vitamin E are depleted, the body is both under oxidative attack and stripped of its defense arsenal. This pattern maps to Defense operation overwhelm: the system is burning through antioxidant reserves faster than they can be replenished, and oxidative damage is accumulating in both nuclear DNA and mitochondrial membranes. [INTERPRETATION] Left unaddressed, this constellation drives accelerated cellular aging, mitochondrial dysfunction (damaged membranes lose electron transport chain efficiency), and increased cancer risk (DNA adducts from unrepaired 8-OHdG lesions). [FACT — 8-OHdG is an established biomarker of oxidative DNA damage; elevated levels are associated with carcinogenesis in epidemiological studies]

### Pattern Recognition

- **The "Paradox of Plenty" pattern:** Multiple nutrient deficiencies in a well-nourished individual. This pattern almost always points upstream to Transduction failure — the gut is not converting dietary input into absorbable micronutrients. Investigate: SIBO, celiac serology, pancreatic elastase, H. pylori, PPI use, chronic NSAID exposure, IgA deficiency. [INTERPRETATION]

- **The "Methylation Bottleneck" pattern:** Elevated MMA + elevated FIGlu + elevated homocysteine + low SAMe (if measured). This triad indicates simultaneous B12 and folate functional deficiency with impaired methylation cycle output, affecting an estimated 200+ methyl-transfer reactions including DNA methylation, neurotransmitter clearance (COMT), phospholipid synthesis, and detoxification conjugation. [FACT — the methylation cycle turns over approximately 3–4 grams of methyl groups per day in a healthy adult] Check MTHFR status and dietary methyl donor adequacy (choline, betaine, creatine).

- **The "Energy Crisis" pattern:** Elevated Krebs cycle organic acids (citric, succinic, fumaric, malic acid) + low CoQ10 + low carnitine + elevated lactate. This constellation reads as Synthesis operation failure at the mitochondrial level — the electron transport chain is stalling, ATP production is inefficient, and the body is shunting toward anaerobic glycolysis. [INTERPRETATION] The person typically presents with fatigue, brain fog, exercise intolerance, and cold extremities — all downstream consequences of reduced cellular energy production.

- **The "Inflammatory Neurotransmitter Steal" pattern:** Low tryptophan + elevated quinolinic acid + elevated kynurenate + low 5-HIAA (serotonin metabolite). Inflammation (elevated hsCRP or cytokines) activates IDO/TDO enzymes that divert tryptophan away from serotonin production and toward the kynurenine pathway. [FACT] The person develops depression, insomnia, and anxiety not because of a serotonin deficiency per se but because immune activation is stealing the precursor. Treating this with SSRIs alone is like bailing water without patching the hull.

### Intervention Levers

- **Targeted repletion based on functional markers, not serum levels.** If MMA is elevated, B12 is functionally deficient regardless of what serum B12 reads. Methylcobalamin or hydroxocobalamin 1,000–5,000 µg/day sublingually bypasses absorption barriers. If FIGlu is elevated, methylfolate (5-MTHF) 400–800 µg/day is preferred over folic acid, particularly in MTHFR variant carriers. If xanthurenate is elevated, pyridoxal-5-phosphate (active B6) 25–50 mg/day. [FACT — these are standard functional medicine repletion protocols]

- **Fatty acid rebalancing.** If omega-6/omega-3 ratio >5:1, aggressive omega-3 supplementation: EPA/DHA 2–4 g/day combined, sourced from triglyceride-form fish oil or algal oil. Simultaneously reduce seed oil consumption (soybean, corn, canola, sunflower) to lower the omega-6 denominator. Target omega-3 index >8%. [FACT — the omega-3 index >8% threshold is associated with the lowest cardiovascular risk in the Framingham Offspring cohort]

- **Antioxidant rescue protocol.** If 8-OHdG and lipid peroxides are elevated with depleted glutathione: NAC (N-acetylcysteine) 600–1,200 mg/day as glutathione precursor, liposomal glutathione 500 mg/day for direct repletion, CoQ10 (ubiquinol form) 200–400 mg/day for mitochondrial membrane protection, vitamin E (mixed tocopherols/tocotrienols) 200–400 IU/day, and alpha-lipoic acid 300–600 mg/day as a universal antioxidant that regenerates both vitamins C and E. [INTERPRETATION — dosing reflects functional medicine consensus; individual RCT evidence varies by marker]

- **Address upstream absorption before downstream repletion.** If multi-nutrient deficiency is present despite adequate intake, supplementing individual nutrients without fixing absorption is a losing strategy. Assess gastric acid production (Heidelberg pH test or betaine HCl challenge), pancreatic exocrine function (fecal elastase-1), small intestinal integrity (lactulose-mannitol permeability test), and gut microbiome composition. A $50 fecal elastase test may explain $1,200 worth of NutrEval deficiencies. [INTERPRETATION]

- **Retest in 90 days, not 30.** Fat-soluble vitamins (D, E, K), cell membrane fatty acids, and intracellular mineral stores take 60–90 days to meaningfully shift. Retesting at 30 days risks discouraging patients with "unchanged" results when the biology simply hasn't had time to respond. [FACT — tissue turnover rates for fat-soluble nutrient stores range from 60–120 days]

### Body Codx Three-Density Reading

**Body:** The NutrEval reads the raw material inventory of the machine. Every operation in the Light Machine requires specific cofactors: Synthesis needs B-vitamins for methylation and amino acids for protein construction; Defense needs glutathione, zinc, and vitamin C for immune cell function; Conduction needs magnesium for nerve impulse propagation and omega-3 fatty acids for myelin membrane integrity; Regulation needs chromium for insulin receptor sensitivity and iodine for thyroid hormone production. A depleted NutrEval is not one broken thing — it is the machine running low on parts, with cascading failures appearing wherever the most stressed operation exhausts its reserves first. The body density reading of the NutrEval asks: does this machine have what it needs to run its operations, or is it rationing? [INTERPRETATION]

**Soul:** What you take in and what you can use are different questions. The NutrEval's deepest soul-density reading is the gap between intake and absorption — between what the world offers and what the person can actually metabolize. A person surrounded by richness who cannot absorb it — nutritionally, relationally, existentially — is living the same Transduction failure at every density. The "Paradox of Plenty" pattern on the NutrEval often mirrors a soul-level pattern: the person eats well, works hard, has resources, and still feels depleted. Not because there isn't enough coming in, but because something in the middle — the converting apparatus — is impaired. At soul density, this apparatus is the meaning-making function: can this person take raw experience and convert it into nourishment? Or does everything pass through without landing? [INTERPRETATION]

**Spirit:** The NutrEval is the most literal inventory of what this particular body arrived encoded to need. Nutritional requirements are not universal — they are expressions of the biological encoding layer. Genetic polymorphisms in MTHFR, COMT, VDR, FADS1/2, FUT2, and hundreds of other genes create a unique nutritional fingerprint that defines what this body requires to express its design. When the NutrEval reveals deficiency, it is not just a clinical finding — it is evidence of a gap between the body's encoded requirements and what the environment is providing. At spirit density, the NutrEval asks: is this body receiving the specific substrate it was designed to run on? Or is it being fed a generic diet that satisfies population averages while starving this particular design? The encoded body has specific fuel requirements. The NutrEval reads whether those requirements are being met. [INTERPRETATION]

### Clinical Pearl

The NutrEval is the most comprehensive single-order functional panel available, but its diagnostic power is wasted without a systematic reading strategy. Do not read the NutrEval as 125 separate results. Read it as a machine status report: start with the organic acids (are the metabolic engines running?), then the oxidative stress markers (is the machine under fire?), then the amino acids (does the building operation have raw materials?), then the fatty acids (are the membranes intact?), then the vitamins and minerals (are the cofactors stocked?). This sequence reads downstream consequences first and upstream causes last — because a person with mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and amino acid depletion who fixates on a low vitamin D level is treating the leaf while the root rots. The NutrEval's power is not its breadth — it is the cross-operational pattern recognition that breadth enables. [INTERPRETATION]

## Organic Acids Test (OAT)

**Body Codx Operation:** Transduction
**What It Measures:** 76 organic acids reflecting energy production, amino acid metabolism, B-vitamin cofactor status, neurotransmitter turnover, fatty acid oxidation efficiency, dysbiosis-produced metabolites, and detoxification capacity.
**Sample Type:** Urine (first morning void or 2-hour random collection)
**Fasting Required:** No
**Lab Provider:** Mosaic Diagnostics (formerly Great Plains Laboratory); Genova Diagnostics (Organix panel)
**Test Code:** MD-OAT (Mosaic); GDX-ORG (Genova)
**Estimated Cost:** $300–$500
**Insurance Coverage:** Rarely covered; some functional medicine plans reimburse partially
**Turnaround Time:** 7–14 business days
**Availability:** Physician order or select DTC channels

### What It Measures

The Organic Acids Test measures 76 organic acids — the intermediate byproducts of cellular metabolism — that accumulate in the urine when enzymatic pathways are impaired, cofactors are depleted, or abnormal microbial metabolism is producing compounds the body must clear. [FACT] Organic acids are water-soluble compounds produced during the metabolism of carbohydrates, fats, amino acids, and neurotransmitters. Under normal conditions, these intermediates are rapidly converted to their downstream products by specific enzymes that require specific vitamin and mineral cofactors. When the enzyme is sluggish — due to cofactor deficiency, genetic polymorphism, toxic inhibition, or substrate overload — the intermediate accumulates, spills into the urine, and becomes measurable. [FACT]

The OAT reads metabolism the way an emissions test reads an engine. You do not need to disassemble the engine to know if it is running clean. You measure what comes out of the tailpipe. [INTERPRETATION] Elevated citric acid, succinic acid, fumaric acid, or malic acid in the urine means the Krebs cycle is stalling — intermediates are backing up because the next enzyme in the cycle lacks its cofactor or the mitochondrial membrane is damaged. [FACT] Elevated 3-hydroxypropionate or methylmalonate means propionate metabolism is impaired, typically from B12 deficiency or propionic acid-producing gut bacteria. [FACT] Elevated quinolinate means the kynurenine pathway is overactive, typically from inflammation-driven IDO/TDO activation diverting tryptophan away from serotonin synthesis. [FACT] Each organic acid is a metabolic telegram — a message from a specific enzymatic reaction reporting its functional status.

The test divides into seven major categories: (1) **Krebs cycle intermediates** — citric, cis-aconitic, isocitric, alpha-ketoglutaric, succinic, fumaric, malic, hydroxymethylglutaric acids, reading mitochondrial energy production efficiency; (2) **Fatty acid oxidation markers** — adipic, suberic, ethylmalonic acids, reading whether the body can burn fat for fuel; (3) **B-vitamin functional markers** — methylmalonic acid (B12), formiminoglutamic acid (folate), xanthurenate/kynurenate (B6), alpha-ketoisovaleric/alpha-ketoisocaproic/alpha-keto-beta-methylvaleric acids (thiamine-dependent), reading cofactor adequacy at the enzymatic level; (4) **Neurotransmitter metabolites** — homovanillic acid/HVA (dopamine), vanillylmandelic acid/VMA (norepinephrine/epinephrine), 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid/5-HIAA (serotonin), quinolinic acid (kynurenine pathway), reading neurotransmitter turnover and routing; (5) **Dysbiosis markers** — D-arabinitol (yeast metabolite), HPHPA/4-cresol (Clostridial metabolite), D-lactic acid (SIBO/bacterial), benzoic acid, hippuric acid (bacterial metabolites), reading gut microbial ecology through its metabolic output; (6) **Oxalate markers** — glyceric, glycolic, oxalic acids, reading endogenous oxalate production and fungal contribution; (7) **Detoxification/oxidative stress markers** — pyroglutamic acid (glutathione turnover), 2-hydroxybutyric acid (oxidative stress), reading hepatic conjugation capacity and redox balance. [FACT]

### Ranges

Organic acids are reported as µg/mg creatinine (normalized to urine concentration). Ranges vary by laboratory and marker. The critical interpretive framework:

| OAT Category | Key Markers | Optimal (Functional) | Elevated Signals |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Krebs Cycle** | Citric, Succinic, Fumaric, Malic acid | Low-normal range (lab-specific) | Mitochondrial inefficiency — enzyme blockade or cofactor depletion [FACT] |
| **Fatty Acid Oxidation** | Adipic, Suberic, Ethylmalonic acid | Low-normal range | Impaired beta-oxidation — carnitine deficiency, CPT-I/II dysfunction [FACT] |
| **B-Vitamin Function** | MMA, FIGlu, Xanthurenate | MMA <3.8; FIGlu <2.0; Xanthurenate <0.7 | B12, folate, or B6 functional deficiency respectively [FACT] |
| **Neurotransmitters** | HVA, VMA, 5-HIAA, Quinolinate | Mid-range (balanced turnover) | High = rapid turnover/excessive breakdown; Low = depleted production [INTERPRETATION] |
| **Dysbiosis** | D-Arabinitol, HPHPA, D-Lactic acid | Low or undetectable | Yeast overgrowth, Clostridial overgrowth, or SIBO respectively [FACT] |
| **Oxalates** | Glyceric, Glycolic, Oxalic acid | Low-normal range | Endogenous overproduction, dietary excess, or fungal contribution [FACT] |
| **Detox/Oxidative Stress** | Pyroglutamic acid, 2-Hydroxybutyric acid | Low-normal range | Glutathione depletion, hepatic oxidative burden [INTERPRETATION] |

### What Deviation Signals

**Elevated Krebs cycle intermediates** are the OAT's most direct window into mitochondrial function — or dysfunction. [FACT] When succinic acid, fumaric acid, and malic acid are simultaneously elevated, the Krebs cycle is backing up at multiple points, indicating either generalized cofactor depletion (the cycle requires thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, iron, magnesium, and manganese at various steps) or mitochondrial membrane damage reducing electron transport chain pull. [INTERPRETATION] The clinical presentation is fatigue — deep, cellular fatigue that is not resolved by rest because the problem is not exertion but energy production efficiency. The body synthesizes approximately 40–70 kg of ATP daily; when the Krebs cycle stalls, ATP production drops and every downstream operation is rationed. [FACT] This is the Synthesis operation failing at its most fundamental level. Add an elevated lactate:pyruvate ratio and the picture sharpens: the body is shunting toward anaerobic glycolysis because oxidative phosphorylation is unreliable.

**Abnormal neurotransmitter metabolites** reveal the routing of mood, motivation, and cognition with a precision that no psychiatric symptom questionnaire can match. [INTERPRETATION] HVA (homovanillic acid) is the terminal metabolite of dopamine; VMA (vanillylmandelic acid) is the terminal metabolite of norepinephrine and epinephrine; 5-HIAA (5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid) is the terminal metabolite of serotonin. Elevated HVA suggests high dopamine turnover — either excessive production or excessive degradation (COMT fast-metabolizer status). Low 5-HIAA with elevated quinolinic acid is the signature of immune-driven tryptophan steal: inflammation activates indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase (IDO), which diverts tryptophan from the serotonin pathway into the kynurenine pathway, producing the neurotoxic quinolinic acid at the expense of the mood-stabilizing serotonin. [FACT] This pattern explains why anti-depressants may fail in the context of systemic inflammation — the problem is not a serotonin reuptake issue but a serotonin production issue caused by precursor diversion.

**Elevated dysbiosis markers** read the gut microbiome's metabolic output without requiring a stool test. [INTERPRETATION] D-arabinitol is a sugar alcohol produced primarily by Candida species; elevated urinary D-arabinitol is a validated marker of invasive and mucosal candidiasis. [FACT] HPHPA (3-(3-hydroxyphenyl)-3-hydroxypropionic acid) and 4-cresol are metabolites produced by Clostridium difficile and related species; their presence indicates overgrowth of these pathogenic organisms in the intestinal tract. [FACT] These Clostridial metabolites are clinically significant because they inhibit dopamine-beta-hydroxylase, the enzyme that converts dopamine to norepinephrine — meaning Clostridial overgrowth can directly alter catecholamine balance and present as behavioral and mood disturbance. [FACT — first described in urine of children with autism; subsequently confirmed in broader populations] Elevated D-lactic acid suggests small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) with lactate-producing organisms fermenting unabsorbed carbohydrates. [FACT]

### Pattern Recognition

- **The "Metabolic Gridlock" pattern:** Elevated Krebs cycle intermediates at 3+ points + elevated lactate + low-normal CoQ10 + elevated pyroglutamic acid. This is the full mitochondrial dysfunction constellation: the energy production pathway is stalling, the electron transport chain is leaking, anaerobic glycolysis is compensating, and glutathione is being consumed faster than it can be regenerated. The person presents as exhausted, foggy, exercise-intolerant, and often cold. The Synthesis operation is in triage. [INTERPRETATION]

- **The "Gut-Brain Axis Hijack" pattern:** Elevated HPHPA/4-cresol (Clostridial) + abnormal HVA:VMA ratio + elevated quinolinate + low 5-HIAA. The gut microbiome is directly interfering with neurotransmitter metabolism through three simultaneous mechanisms: Clostridial metabolites inhibiting dopamine-beta-hydroxylase, inflammatory tryptophan diversion via the kynurenine pathway, and bacterial endotoxin (LPS) activating central neuroinflammation. [INTERPRETATION] The mood disorder is downstream of the gut disorder. This pattern is especially common in post-antibiotic states, chronic GI dysfunction, and autism-spectrum presentations.

- **The "Hidden B-Vitamin Famine" pattern:** Elevated MMA + elevated FIGlu + elevated xanthurenate + elevated alpha-keto acids. All four B-vitamin functional markers are elevated simultaneously, indicating multi-B-vitamin functional deficiency. Standard serum B12, folate, and B6 levels may be "normal" — the OAT is reading functional status at the enzymatic level, not circulating supply. [FACT — serum B12 has a sensitivity of only ~50% for detecting functional B12 deficiency when MMA is used as the gold standard] This pattern may reflect poor dietary quality, malabsorption, or genetic variants affecting cofactor activation (MTHFR, MTRR, NBPF3).

- **The "Yeast-Oxalate-Oxidative Triad":** Elevated D-arabinitol + elevated oxalic acid + elevated pyroglutamic acid. Candida overgrowth produces oxalic acid as a metabolic byproduct; oxalate itself depletes glutathione through direct chemical reaction; glutathione depletion appears as elevated pyroglutamic acid. [HYPOTHESIS — the causal chain from Candida → oxalate → glutathione depletion is mechanistically plausible but incompletely validated in controlled studies] This self-reinforcing loop can persist until the fungal overgrowth is addressed directly.

### Intervention Levers

- **Mitochondrial rescue for elevated Krebs cycle markers.** CoQ10 (ubiquinol) 200–400 mg/day restores electron transport chain efficiency; L-carnitine or acetyl-L-carnitine 1,000–2,000 mg/day facilitates fatty acid transport into mitochondria for beta-oxidation; riboflavin (B2) 25–50 mg/day supports Complex I and II; niacin/niacinamide (B3) 50–100 mg/day supports NAD+ regeneration; alpha-lipoic acid 300–600 mg/day regenerates NADH and serves as mitochondrial antioxidant. [INTERPRETATION — dosing reflects clinical practice; individual efficacy data varies by context] Concurrent magnesium repletion (glycinate or threonate form, 400–600 mg/day elemental) is often necessary, as magnesium is a required cofactor for multiple Krebs cycle enzymes. [FACT]

- **Dysbiosis-targeted antimicrobial protocols.** For elevated D-arabinitol (yeast): antifungal agents — pharmaceutical (fluconazole, nystatin) or botanical (oregano oil with >70% carvacrol, caprylic acid 1,000–2,000 mg/day, berberine 500 mg 2–3x/day) combined with biofilm disruptors (NAC, enzymes) and Saccharomyces boulardii as a competitive probiotic. For elevated HPHPA/4-cresol (Clostridial): targeted antibiotics (vancomycin oral, metronidazole) or botanical protocols (allicin from garlic, olive leaf extract) followed by competitive re-colonization with Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species. [INTERPRETATION — botanical antimicrobial protocols are widely used in functional medicine but have limited RCT data; pharmaceutical antimicrobials are Tier 1 for specific organisms]

- **Neurotransmitter precursor support guided by metabolite routing.** If 5-HIAA is low with elevated quinolinate, the serotonin pathway is being starved of precursor. Support with 5-HTP (50–200 mg/day) to bypass the IDO bottleneck — 5-HTP enters the serotonin pathway downstream of the tryptophan → 5-HTP step that IDO diverts. [FACT — 5-HTP is a direct serotonin precursor that bypasses IDO-mediated diversion] Simultaneously address the inflammation driving IDO activation (omega-3 fatty acids, curcumin, SPM supplementation). If HVA is elevated with low VMA, support dopamine-beta-hydroxylase with vitamin C (1,000–2,000 mg/day) and copper (cofactors for DBH). [FACT]

- **B-vitamin repletion by functional marker, not serum level.** The OAT's B-vitamin functional markers are more sensitive than serum levels for guiding repletion. MMA elevated → B12 (methylcobalamin or hydroxocobalamin, sublingual or injectable); FIGlu elevated → methylfolate (5-MTHF, not folic acid); xanthurenate elevated → pyridoxal-5-phosphate (P5P, active B6); alpha-keto acids elevated → thiamine (benfotiamine preferred for superior bioavailability). [FACT — functional markers guide targeted repletion more precisely than empiric multivitamin approaches]

- **Oxalate reduction protocol.** If oxalic acid is elevated: reduce dietary oxalate (spinach, almonds, sweet potatoes, chocolate are high-oxalate foods), supplement calcium citrate with meals (calcium binds oxalate in the gut, preventing absorption), support gut-based Oxalobacter formigenes colonization (this organism degrades oxalate in the intestinal lumen), and assess for Candida contribution (if D-arabinitol is co-elevated, treat the yeast first). [INTERPRETATION — Oxalobacter colonization support is emerging but not yet standard practice]

### Body Codx Three-Density Reading

**Body:** The OAT reads the exhaust of the metabolic engine. Organic acids are what the cell produces when it is working — and what accumulates when it is struggling. Every elevated marker is a stalled enzymatic reaction reporting its status through the urine. This is the most direct window into Transduction available without a tissue biopsy: how efficiently is this body converting substrate into energy, neurotransmitter, structural protein, and waste? The OAT does not measure the nutrient — it measures the metabolic consequence of nutrient presence or absence. A normal serum B12 with an elevated MMA is the body saying: "I have the supply, but the conversion isn't happening." That distinction — between supply and conversion — is the essence of the Transduction operation. [INTERPRETATION]

**Soul:** The OAT reveals what the body is actually doing with what it receives — not what it has, but what it makes. At soul density, this reads as the person's capacity for transformation: can they take raw experience and metabolize it into something usable? The Krebs cycle — the body's central metabolic pathway — is a cycle of transformation. Substrate enters, is broken down, releases energy, and the cycle begins again. When this cycle stalls biochemically, look for the parallel at soul density: is the person stuck in a loop where raw experience enters but never gets processed into wisdom, growth, or meaning? The elevated organic acid is the unmetabolized experience sitting in the system, neither used nor eliminated. The person who says "I keep going through the same thing but nothing changes" is describing the soul-density equivalent of elevated Krebs cycle intermediates. [INTERPRETATION]

**Spirit:** At spirit density, the OAT reads the integrity of the transformation process itself — the capacity for one thing to become another. The Krebs cycle is the body's purest expression of the alchemical principle: matter is consumed, energy is released, the cycle renews. Every organic acid marker is a point in this transformation sequence, and every elevation is a point where transformation has stalled. The spirit-density question is not "which cofactor is missing?" but "where in this system has the capacity for transformation itself been interrupted?" Stuck transformation — the inability to let the old become fuel for the new — manifests as biochemical gridlock at body density, psychological stuckness at soul density, and spiritual stagnation at spirit density. The OAT catches the body's version. The facilitator reads the spirit's version. They are the same process at different resolutions. [INTERPRETATION]

### Clinical Pearl

The OAT's greatest clinical value is that it reads functional metabolism in a single urine collection, without the need for fasting, venipuncture, or expensive blood panels. But its greatest interpretive danger is treating each organic acid as an isolated finding. A single elevated marker is a clue; a pattern of elevated markers across categories is a diagnosis. Read the OAT in clusters: Krebs cycle intermediates together (energy production), neurotransmitter metabolites together (brain chemistry routing), dysbiosis markers together (microbial ecology), B-vitamin markers together (cofactor status). The clinician who reads the OAT as 76 separate numbers will miss the cross-category patterns that are the test's real diagnostic power — particularly the gut-brain axis patterns where dysbiosis markers and neurotransmitter metabolites fire in concert, revealing that the "psychiatric" symptom has a gastrointestinal origin. [INTERPRETATION]

---

## Metabolomix+

**Body Codx Operation:** Transduction
**What It Measures:** Organic acids, amino acids, fatty acids, and oxidative stress markers — a urine-based comprehensive metabolic assessment combining three historically separate panels into a single collection.
**Sample Type:** Urine (first morning void)
**Fasting Required:** No
**Lab Provider:** Genova Diagnostics
**Test Code:** GDX-MX
**Estimated Cost:** $600–$900
**Insurance Coverage:** Rarely covered; partial reimbursement through some functional medicine plans
**Turnaround Time:** 10–14 business days
**Availability:** Physician order required

### What It Measures

The Metabolomix+ is Genova Diagnostics' integrated metabolic profile — a single urine collection that quantifies 76+ metabolites spanning organic acids, amino acids, fatty acid metabolites, and oxidative stress markers using liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). [FACT] It combines what were historically three separate test orders (organic acids panel, amino acid profile, fatty acid oxidation markers) into one specimen. The test reads the net metabolic state of the organism by capturing the downstream products of hepatic, mitochondrial, and microbial metabolic activity over a 24-hour urinary collection window.

Where the standalone OAT reads primarily organic acid intermediates, the Metabolomix+ adds two critical dimensions: plasma-free amino acid equivalents measured in urine (reflecting amino acid utilization efficiency, protein digestive adequacy, and anabolic capacity) and oxidative stress markers including 8-hydroxy-2-deoxyguanosine (8-OHdG), which is the gold-standard urinary marker of oxidative DNA damage. [FACT — 8-OHdG is produced when reactive oxygen species attack guanine residues in DNA; urinary 8-OHdG reflects whole-body oxidative DNA damage repair rate] This makes the Metabolomix+ a more complete metabolic snapshot than the OAT alone, while remaining less comprehensive than the NutrEval (which adds blood-based vitamin, mineral, and fatty acid quantification).

The Metabolomix+ divides into five reporting sections: (1) **Organic acids** — Krebs cycle intermediates (citric, cis-aconitic, isocitric, alpha-ketoglutaric, succinic, fumaric, malic), fatty acid oxidation markers (adipic, suberic, ethylmalonic), B-vitamin cofactor markers (MMA, FIGlu, xanthurenate), neurotransmitter metabolites (HVA, VMA, 5-HIAA, kynurenate, quinolinate), and detoxification/sulfation markers (pyroglutamic acid, alpha-hydroxybutyrate, 2-methylhippurate, orotate); (2) **Amino acids** — essential and non-essential amino acid profiles including branched-chain amino acids (leucine, isoleucine, valine), aromatic amino acids (phenylalanine, tyrosine, tryptophan), sulfur amino acids (methionine, cysteine, taurine, homocysteine), and conditionally essential amino acids (glutamine, arginine, glycine); (3) **Fatty acid metabolites** — markers reflecting essential fatty acid status and beta-oxidation efficiency, including adipic and suberic acids as indicators of impaired long-chain fatty acid catabolism; (4) **Oxidative stress markers** — 8-OHdG (DNA damage), lipid peroxides, and pyroglutamic acid (glutathione depletion); (5) **Dysbiosis markers** — D-arabinitol, HPHPA, D-lactic acid, benzoate, hippurate. [FACT]

### Ranges

Like the OAT, Metabolomix+ results are reported as µg/mg creatinine for organic acids and µmol/mg creatinine for amino acids. Genova provides a graphical report with values plotted against functional reference ranges.

| Metabolomix+ Section | Key Markers | Optimal Range | Clinical Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Krebs Cycle** | Citric, Succinic, Fumaric, Malic acid | Lower half of reference range | Any >2x upper limit of reference = significant mitochondrial stress [INTERPRETATION] |
| **Amino Acids (Essential)** | Leucine, Isoleucine, Valine, Tryptophan, Methionine | Mid-range | Low essential AAs = protein maldigestion or intake deficiency [FACT] |
| **Amino Acids (Conditionally Essential)** | Glutamine, Glycine, Arginine | Mid to upper range | Low glutamine = gut barrier stress; Low glycine = impaired conjugation [INTERPRETATION] |
| **Neurotransmitter Metabolites** | HVA, VMA, 5-HIAA, Quinolinate | Mid-range (balanced turnover) | See OAT interpretation above [FACT] |
| **Oxidative Stress** | 8-OHdG | <5.0 ng/mg creatinine | >8.0 = significant oxidative DNA damage burden [INTERPRETATION] |
| **Dysbiosis** | D-Arabinitol, HPHPA | Low or undetectable | Any elevation = pathogenic overgrowth [FACT] |
| **B-Vitamin Function** | MMA, FIGlu, Xanthurenate | See OAT ranges above | See OAT interpretation above [FACT] |

### What Deviation Signals

**Amino acid imbalances** are the Metabolomix+ dimension that the standalone OAT misses, and they are frequently the most clinically actionable findings. [INTERPRETATION] Low branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs: leucine, isoleucine, valine) indicate impaired anabolic capacity — the body lacks the building blocks for skeletal muscle protein synthesis, mTOR-mediated repair signaling, and glucose disposal via muscle glycogen storage. [FACT — BCAAs activate mTORC1, the master regulator of anabolic protein synthesis] Low BCAAs in a person who consumes adequate protein point upstream to maldigestion: insufficient gastric acid, pancreatic protease deficiency, or brush border peptidase dysfunction. Low tryptophan specifically — particularly when paired with elevated kynurenine metabolites on the organic acids section — confirms the inflammatory tryptophan steal pattern described above, but now with the direct amino acid level to corroborate the metabolite routing evidence. Low sulfur amino acids (methionine, cysteine, taurine) signal impaired methylation and transsulfuration capacity, with downstream consequences for glutathione production, bile acid conjugation (taurine), and hepatic detoxification. [FACT]

**Elevated 8-OHdG** is the Metabolomix+ result that should generate the most clinical urgency when significantly elevated. [INTERPRETATION] 8-OHdG is formed when reactive oxygen species (specifically hydroxyl radical) attack the C8 position of guanine in DNA. The damaged guanine is excised by base excision repair (OGG1 enzyme) and excreted in urine. Urinary 8-OHdG therefore reflects the rate of oxidative DNA damage occurring across every cell in the body. [FACT] Elevated 8-OHdG has been associated in epidemiological studies with increased risk of cancer (particularly breast, lung, colorectal, and hepatocellular carcinoma), cardiovascular disease, neurodegenerative disease, and accelerated aging. [FACT — multiple large cohort studies confirm the association; the CARDIA study and European prospective studies are among the most cited] When 8-OHdG is elevated alongside depleted glutathione markers (elevated pyroglutamic acid) and depleted antioxidant cofactors (low CoQ10, low vitamin E on NutrEval if available), the system is both producing excessive oxidative damage and failing to neutralize it — a biochemical emergency at the level of genomic integrity.

**The amino acid–organic acid convergence** is the Metabolomix+ distinctive diagnostic power. [INTERPRETATION] Because it measures both amino acids and their metabolic products simultaneously, the Metabolomix+ can distinguish between deficient supply and deficient conversion. For example: normal plasma tryptophan with elevated quinolinate indicates that tryptophan supply is adequate but is being diverted to the kynurenine pathway (conversion problem — inflammation). Low plasma tryptophan with low 5-HIAA indicates that tryptophan supply itself is insufficient (intake or absorption problem). Normal tryptophan with low 5-HIAA and normal kynurenine metabolites indicates impaired tryptophan hydroxylase activity (enzyme dysfunction — may be cofactor-related; TPH requires iron and BH4). The same amino acid, three different metabolite patterns, three different root causes, three different interventions. This is why the combined panel is diagnostically superior to either section alone.

### Pattern Recognition

- **The "Catabolic State" pattern:** Low BCAAs + low glutamine + low arginine + elevated 3-methylhistidine (if included). The body is consuming its own structural protein because anabolic input cannot keep pace with catabolic demand. This may reflect chronic illness, overtraining, chronic stress (cortisol-driven proteolysis), inadequate protein intake, or malabsorption. The Synthesis operation is running a deficit — it is breaking down more structure than it is building. [INTERPRETATION]

- **The "Toxic Burden Decompensation" pattern:** Elevated pyroglutamic acid + elevated 2-hydroxybutyric acid + elevated orotate + low glycine + low taurine. Multiple detoxification pathways are simultaneously overwhelmed: glutathione is depleted (pyroglutamic), hepatic Phase I is producing reactive intermediates faster than Phase II can conjugate them (2-hydroxybutyric), the urea cycle is backing up (orotate), and glycine and taurine conjugation substrates are exhausted. The Elimination operation is failing not because the waste isn't being produced but because the conjugation-and-excretion machinery has run out of substrate. [INTERPRETATION]

- **The "Metabolic Bottleneck Cascade" pattern:** Elevated Krebs cycle intermediates + low BCAAs + elevated ammonia (from amino acid catabolism) + elevated oxidative stress markers. Energy production is failing, the body is catabolizing amino acids as alternative fuel, generating excess ammonia that must be cleared through the urea cycle, and the resulting metabolic stress is producing oxidative damage that further impairs mitochondrial function. This is a self-amplifying loop: mitochondrial dysfunction → amino acid catabolism → ammonia burden → urea cycle stress → further mitochondrial damage. [INTERPRETATION]

- **The "Neurotransmitter Substrate Famine" pattern:** Low tryptophan + low tyrosine + low phenylalanine + abnormal HVA/VMA/5-HIAA. The brain's raw materials for producing serotonin (tryptophan → 5-HTP → serotonin), dopamine (tyrosine → L-DOPA → dopamine), and norepinephrine (dopamine → norepinephrine) are all depleted at the amino acid level. Neurotransmitter metabolites confirm the downstream consequence. This pattern responds to amino acid precursor therapy far better than to receptor-targeted pharmaceuticals, because the problem is not receptor sensitivity but production substrate. [INTERPRETATION]

### Intervention Levers

- **Amino acid repletion protocols guided by the profile.** Free-form essential amino acid complexes (EAAs) at 10–15 g/day provide the building blocks the body is missing without requiring full protein digestion. For specific deficiencies: BCAAs 5–10 g/day for low leucine/isoleucine/valine; L-glutamine 5–15 g/day for depleted glutamine (gut barrier repair, immune cell fuel); glycine 3–5 g/day for depleted glycine (detoxification conjugation, collagen synthesis, glutathione production); taurine 1–3 g/day for depleted taurine (bile conjugation, cardiac function, neuronal stabilization). [INTERPRETATION — dosing reflects clinical practice consensus; RCT evidence varies by amino acid and condition]

- **Digestive optimization upstream of supplementation.** If essential amino acids are low despite adequate protein intake, the problem is digestion, not diet. Betaine HCl with pepsin (one capsule escalating to 3–5 per protein-containing meal) restores gastric acid for protein denaturation; digestive enzyme complex with protease, lipase, and amylase supports pancreatic exocrine function; bitters (gentian, artichoke extract) before meals stimulate endogenous digestive secretion. [INTERPRETATION — the betaine HCl challenge test is widely used in functional medicine but lacks rigorous RCT validation; digestive enzyme supplementation has more robust evidence for pancreatic insufficiency]

- **Oxidative stress management when 8-OHdG is elevated.** The intervention hierarchy for elevated 8-OHdG: (1) identify and remove the oxidative source (environmental toxins, chronic infection, excessive exercise, chronic psychological stress — all increase ROS production); (2) replete glutathione directly (liposomal glutathione 500 mg/day) and via precursors (NAC 600–1,200 mg/day + glycine 3 g/day — together these supply both cysteine and glycine for the glutathione tripeptide); (3) support antioxidant enzyme systems (selenium 200 µg/day for glutathione peroxidase, zinc 30 mg/day for SOD, manganese for mitochondrial SOD); (4) exogenous antioxidants (vitamin C 1,000–2,000 mg/day, vitamin E mixed tocopherols/tocotrienols 200 IU/day, CoQ10 200 mg/day). [INTERPRETATION — hierarchical approach prioritizing source removal and endogenous defense over exogenous supplementation]

- **Detoxification support for the Toxic Burden pattern.** Glycine repletion (3–5 g/day) to restore Phase II glycine conjugation; taurine repletion (1–3 g/day) for taurine conjugation; calcium-D-glucarate 500–1,500 mg/day to support glucuronidation; glutathione precursors (NAC + glycine) to restore Phase II glutathione conjugation; cruciferous vegetable intake or sulforaphane supplementation (broccoli seed extract) to upregulate Nrf2-mediated Phase II enzyme expression. [INTERPRETATION — Nrf2 induction by sulforaphane is well-characterized in vitro and in clinical trials; dosing for specific clinical outcomes is still being established]

- **Combined amino acid–organic acid guided neurotransmitter support.** When both the amino acid substrate AND the metabolite routing are visible, intervention can be precisely targeted. Low tryptophan → 5-HTP (50–200 mg/day, bypasses IDO). Low tyrosine → L-tyrosine (500–2,000 mg/day, on empty stomach). Low phenylalanine → DL-phenylalanine (500–1,000 mg/day). Always co-supplement with necessary cofactors: B6 (P5P), iron, folate, and vitamin C are required at various enzymatic steps in all three neurotransmitter synthesis pathways. [FACT — cofactor requirements for tryptophan hydroxylase (iron, BH4), tyrosine hydroxylase (iron, BH4, vitamin C), and DOPA decarboxylase (B6) are biochemically established]

### Body Codx Three-Density Reading

**Body:** The Metabolomix+ reads both the substrate pool (amino acids) and the metabolic output (organic acids) simultaneously, giving the clinician a before-and-after snapshot of the Transduction operation. Amino acids are what the body receives after digesting protein. Organic acids are what the body produces after metabolizing those amino acids (and carbohydrates, and fats). By reading both, the Metabolomix+ reveals not just what is low or high, but where in the metabolic pipeline the breakdown is occurring. A low amino acid with a normal organic acid means the substrate never arrived (Reception or Transduction failure upstream). A normal amino acid with an abnormal organic acid means the substrate arrived but the conversion failed (Transduction or Synthesis failure downstream). The Metabolomix+ catches both halves of the equation. [INTERPRETATION]

**Soul:** The Metabolomix+ maps the relationship between what a person takes in and what they produce — between raw material and metabolic output. At soul density, this reads as the person's metabolic identity: given the same raw inputs as anyone else, what does this particular organism make? The amino acid profile is the soul-density signature of nutritional individuality — two people eating the same diet will have different amino acid profiles based on their digestive capacity, genetic enzyme variants, microbiome composition, stress load, and exercise demands. The organic acid profile is the signature of metabolic individuality — two people with the same amino acid pool will produce different metabolic outputs based on their mitochondrial health, cofactor status, and toxic burden. Together, they read not just what is wrong but who this person is metabolically — their unique metabolic fingerprint. [INTERPRETATION]

**Spirit:** At spirit density, the Metabolomix+ reads the alchemical efficiency of the organism — how completely this body transmutes matter into energy, structure, and signal. The Krebs cycle is pure alchemy: carbon-based substrate enters, is systematically deconstructed through eight enzymatic steps, and emerges as CO₂, water, and high-energy electron carriers that power the entire organism. The amino acid → neurotransmitter pathway is equally alchemical: a molecule from food becomes a thought, a mood, a motivation, a dream. The Metabolomix+ reads how faithfully this alchemy is proceeding. Where it stalls — where the organic acid accumulates or the amino acid depletes without producing its downstream product — is where the body's capacity for transformation is interrupted. At spirit density, the question is not "what supplement fixes this?" but "where in this organism has the capacity for transformation itself been compromised?" [INTERPRETATION]

### Clinical Pearl

The Metabolomix+ occupies a strategic middle ground in the functional specialty panel hierarchy: more comprehensive than the standalone OAT (adds amino acids and oxidative stress markers), less comprehensive than the NutrEval (no blood-based vitamin, mineral, or direct fatty acid quantification), and entirely urine-based (no venipuncture required). This makes it the ideal first-line functional panel for patients who are new to advanced testing, needle-averse, or cost-conscious. The amino acid–organic acid convergence it provides is diagnostically unique: no other single collection allows the clinician to see both the raw material pool and its metabolic fate in one report. When the Metabolomix+ reveals patterns that demand deeper investigation — particularly widespread deficiency suggesting malabsorption — escalate to the NutrEval, which adds the blood-based micronutrient quantification necessary to distinguish between intake deficiency and functional conversion failure. The Metabolomix+ asks the question; the NutrEval often supplies the answer. [INTERPRETATION]

---

## Cross-Marker Constellation: The Comprehensive Functional Assessment Hierarchy

**Constellation Name:** Functional Panel Hierarchy
**Operational Focus:** Cross-Operation (Transduction primary, all operations secondary)
**Clinical Significance:** High — panel selection determines diagnostic precision

### Constellation Description

The three panels in this section form a diagnostic hierarchy rather than a menu of equivalent choices. The OAT ($300–$500) is the entry-level functional metabolic screen — 76 organic acids in a single urine collection, reading energy production, neurotransmitter routing, dysbiosis, and B-vitamin functional status. The Metabolomix+ ($600–$900) adds amino acid profiling and oxidative stress markers to the organic acids, enabling the substrate-to-product convergence reading that identifies where in the metabolic pipeline dysfunction occurs. The NutrEval FMV ($800–$1,200) adds blood-based vitamin, mineral, and fatty acid quantification to everything the Metabolomix+ provides, creating the most comprehensive functional assessment available.

**Decision logic:** If the clinical question is "Is there metabolic dysfunction?" → start with OAT. If the clinical question is "Where in the metabolic pipeline is the dysfunction occurring?" → order Metabolomix+. If the clinical question is "What is the complete nutritional and metabolic status of this organism?" → order NutrEval. If cost is not limiting, the NutrEval is always the superior choice because it contains everything the other two panels measure plus direct micronutrient quantification. [INTERPRETATION]

### Encoding Layer Summary

| Encoding Layer | Relevance to Functional Specialty Panels |
|---|---|
| **Biological (Layer 2)** | Genetic polymorphisms in MTHFR, COMT, CBS, CYP enzymes, FADS1/2, FUT2, and SLC transporters create the unique metabolic fingerprint that determines which organic acids, amino acids, and fatty acids will deviate from population norms. The OAT and Metabolomix+ read the phenotypic expression of these genetic variants. [FACT] |
| **Ancestral (Layer 3)** | Epigenetic modifications inherited transgenerationally — particularly methylation marks — affect the expression of metabolic enzymes. A person with ancestral famine exposure may carry epigenetic marks that upregulate fat storage enzymes and downregulate fatty acid oxidation, producing a specific organic acid pattern (elevated adipic/suberic acids) generations later. [HYPOTHESIS — transgenerational epigenetic inheritance of metabolic patterns is established in animal models; human evidence is accumulating but not yet definitive] |
| **Developmental (Layer 4)** | Early-life nutrition, antibiotic exposure, and microbial colonization patterns establish the gut microbiome ecology that will produce the dysbiosis markers on these panels for decades. A single course of broad-spectrum antibiotics in the first year of life can permanently alter Clostridial and Candida colonization patterns that later appear as elevated HPHPA, 4-cresol, and D-arabinitol on the OAT. [FACT — epidemiological data confirms lasting microbiome alterations from early-life antibiotic exposure; the specificity of the organic acid patterns is interpretive] |
| **Cultural (Layer 6)** | Standard American Diet (SAD) — high in processed carbohydrates, seed oils, and food additives, low in organ meats, fermented foods, and diverse fiber — produces a predictable pattern on these panels: elevated omega-6/omega-3 ratio, depleted B-vitamins (processing destroys thiamine, riboflavin, and B6), low glutamine and glycine (minimal collagen/bone broth intake), and elevated dysbiosis markers (processed food selects for pathogenic gut flora). The "average" American functional panel is not normal — it is the metabolic signature of a culturally installed diet. [INTERPRETATION] |
| **Chosen (Layer 8)** | Therapeutic interventions — targeted supplementation, dietary modification, antimicrobial protocols, stress management — are measurable on 90-day retesting. The Metabolomix+ and NutrEval provide the clearest before-and-after documentation of whether a chosen intervention is producing the intended biochemical change. [FACT] |
